1: A type: "When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like those who dream." The restoration that surpasses imagination — the disciples' reaction to the resurrection was exactly this: Luke 24:41 — "they still disbelieved for joy and were marveling." The definitive restoration is Christ's resurrection, the firstfruits of the full restoration of God's people (1 Corinthians 15:20-23).
2: A type: "our mouth was filled with laughter, and our tongue with shouts of joy." The joy of the nations at Israel's restoration anticipates the eschatological joy of all peoples at the gospel — Acts 11:18 (Gentiles "glorified God, saying, 'Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance that leads to life'"); Luke 15:10 ("there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents").
3: A revelation of God: "The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad." The greatest act of God is the resurrection of Christ from the dead (Ephesians 1:19-20 — "the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, which he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead"). All God's "great things" find their culmination in the Christ-event.
4: A shadow: "Restore our fortunes, O LORD, like streams in the Negeb!" The sudden, abundant streams in the dry desert prefigure the outpouring of the Spirit — John 7:37-38 ("rivers of living water" from those who believe in Christ). The eschatological ingathering: John 11:52 — Christ died "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad"; Matthew 8:11 — many will come from east and west to the kingdom.
5: A type: "Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy." John 12:24 — "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit." The suffering-to-glory pattern — exile-return, sowing-reaping — is the pattern Christ enacts in his death and resurrection. His tears (John 11:35; Luke 19:41) become the resurrection joy (John 20:20).
6: A type: "He who goes out weeping, bearing the seed for sowing, shall come home with shouts of joy, bringing his sheaves with him." The portrait of the sower who weeps and returns with harvest is the portrait of Christ who "went out" to the cross bearing the seed of his own death, and who "came home" in the resurrection with the harvest of redeemed humanity. Hebrews 12:2 — "who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross."
